title: Alchemilla indivisa
taxonomy: Alchemilla indivisa is classified within the family Rosaceae, which includes a wide range of flowering plants such as roses and apples. This plant is also a member of the subfamily Rosoideae, a group that is characterized by having a rosaceous leaf arrangement and a distinctive inflorescence structure. Within the genus Alchemilla, Alchemilla indivisa belongs to the species of the alpine and subalpine zones. It is a type of flowering plant commonly found in mountainous regions of Europe. Alchemilla indivisa is further classified as a perennial plant, indicating that it remains in a vegetative state during the winter months.
uses: Alchemilla indivisa, a plant species found in Eastern Europe and Western Asia, has been studied for its potential medicinal properties. Its leaves and stems contain flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been reported to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. parts: Alchemilla indivisa. The medicinal parts of Alchemilla indivisa, also known as the lesser lady's mantle or alchemilla, are primarily its leaves and roots. intro: Alchemilla indivisa, commonly known as the Lady's Mantle, is a perennial herb belonging to the Rosaceae family. The plant is rich in health benefits, including its ability to reduce menstrual cramps and heavy bleeding, as well as its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Alchemilla indivisa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and tannins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Traditionally, Lady's Mantle is used to make herbal preparations such as infusions, tinctures, and capsules, which are often used to treat menstrual disorders and promote uterine health.
